name: Fenodyree
description: The Fenodyree is a mythical creature from Manx folklore, a type of brownie or hobgoblin known for his immense strength and his willingness to perform arduous tasks for humans. He is often described as a hairy, uncouth being, yet essentially benevolent. While generally helpful, he's also known to be easily offended and prone to mischief if not treated with respect. He is a solitary creature, preferring to work alone and unseen, and his motivations are often tied to a sense of duty or a desire for simple gratitude, rather than monetary reward. Stories about the Fenodyree serve as cautionary tales about the importance of respecting those who help us and the dangers of offering inappropriate or unwanted compensation. He is a powerful figure, deeply connected to the land and its rhythms, and his presence is a reminder of the ancient beliefs and traditions of the Isle of Man. He is a creature of the wild, a guardian of the harvest, and a symbol of the sometimes-unpredictable nature of the natural world. He is a reminder that even the most helpful beings have their limits and deserve respect.
subtitle: The Hairy Helper of Manx Farms
appearance: The Fenodyree is typically described as a large, hairy man, covered in shaggy, dark fur. He is often depicted as being unclothed or wearing only simple rags. His features are rough and unrefined, and he has a wild, untamed appearance. Some accounts suggest he has pointed ears and a tail, further emphasizing his connection to the animal world. Despite his somewhat frightening appearance, he is not inherently malicious.
habitat: The Fenodyree is believed to inhabit rural areas, particularly farms and fields on the Isle of Man. He is often associated with barns, stables, and other agricultural structures where he can perform his tasks unseen. He prefers secluded locations and avoids human contact whenever possible.
behavior: The Fenodyree is generally a helpful creature, willing to perform tasks such as threshing, harvesting, and herding livestock. He works tirelessly through the night, completing jobs that would take humans days to finish. However, he is easily offended by gifts of clothing, which he interprets as a sign that his services are no longer needed. When angered, he can become mischievous and destructive, undoing his previous good deeds. He is also known to be solitary and prefers to work alone.

keyMyths.title: The Gift of Clothes
keyMyths.description: A farmer, feeling sorry for the Fenodyree's lack of clothing, left him a set of clothes. The Fenodyree, insulted by the gesture, abandoned his work and left the farm, lamenting his lost freedom.

sources.author: A.W. Moore
sources.work: The Folk-Lore of the Isle of Man
sources.description: A comprehensive collection of Manx folklore, including detailed accounts of the Fenodyree.
sources.text: A comprehensive collection of Manx folklore, including detailed accounts of the Fenodyree.

sources.author: Sophia Morrison
sources.description: A collection of Manx fairy tales featuring the Fenodyree and other mythical creatures.
